foundation.title: REPEAT
foundation.subtitle: For Loops
foundation.content: Loops allow you to repeat a block of code multiple times without rewriting it. 'For' loops are used when you know exactly how many times you want to repeat.
foundation.scenario: A 100-Meter Dash. You don't tell the runner to 'Step' 100 times. You say 'For every meter, take a step until you reach 100'.

examples.title: The Range Loop
examples.explanation: Repeating code exactly X times using the range() function.

examples.title: Item Traversal
examples.explanation: Going through a list of items and doing something with each one.

examples.title: Math Generation
examples.explanation: Creating a sequence of numbers (like 2, 4, 6...) automatically.

foundation.keyPoint: for <item> in <collection>: runs once for each item.

foundation.title: WHILE
foundation.subtitle: Until Loops
foundation.content: 'While' loops repeat as long as a certain condition is True. They are used when you don't know how long the task will take.
foundation.scenario: Eating a Bowl of Soup. You don't know many spoons it takes. You say 'While the bowl is not empty, keep eating'.

examples.title: The Forever Loop
examples.explanation: Running a task until a specific event (like 'Game Over') happens.

examples.title: Input Wait
examples.explanation: Asking the user for input until they give a valid answer.

examples.title: Status Watch
examples.explanation: Waiting for a background process to finish its job.

foundation.keyPoint: Ensure the condition eventually becomes False to avoid infinite loops!

foundation.title: MATH
foundation.subtitle: Arithmetic Power
foundation.content: Python is a powerful calculator. It can handle massive numbers and complex formulas with simple symbols like +, -, *, and /.
foundation.scenario: A Checkout Counter. Adding item prices (+), applying discounts (-), multiplying for bulk quantities (*), and dividing the bill between friends (/).

examples.title: Basic Calculus
examples.explanation: Standard math operators (+, -, *, /) work exactly as in school.

examples.title: The Power Op
examples.explanation: Using ** to calculate exponents (like 10 to the power of 2).

examples.title: Modulo Check
examples.explanation: Using % to find the remainder. If x % 2 is 0, the number is even.

gameData.hint: A condition that is always true.
